The call that Divine grace intends to be effective will be repeated until it is, until we respond to the call. Eli, realizing that Samuel heard the voice of God, instructed him what to say. Although it was shameful for Eli, for God’s call to go to Samuel, he told him how to respond to it. Thus, elders should do their best to assist and improve those who are coming up. Let us never fail to teach those who will come after us, even those who will soon be preferred over us. Good words should be put into children's mouths early so they can be prepared to learn about God and be raised to respect them.

How much guilt and corruption there is in us, concerning which we may say, It is the sin that our own heart knows; we are conscious of it! Those who do not restrain the sins of others when they have the power to do so make themselves partakers of the guilt and will be charged as joining in it. In his remarkable answer to this awful sentence, Eli acknowledged that the Lord had a right to do as he saw good, being assured that he would do nothing wrong. The meekness, patience, and humility contained in those words show that he was truly repentant; he accepted the punishment for his sin.

All increase in wisdom and grace is due to God’s presence with us. God will graciously repeat his visits to those who receive them properly. Early piety will be the greatest honor of young people. Those who honor God, he will honor. Let young people consider Samuel’s piety, and from him they will learn to remember their Creator in the days of their youth. Young children are capable of religion. Samuel is proof that their waiting upon the Lord will be pleasing to him. He is a pattern of all those amiable tempers which are the brightest ornament of youth and a sure source of happiness.
